coredata.dc:title: Engineering white light-emitting diodes with high color rendering index from biomass carbonized polymer dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Weixue
Wang, Boyang
Ai, Lin
Song, Haoqiang
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have promise in the fields of sensing, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices due to their excellent optical properties, favorable biocompatibility, and superior stability. Biomass CPDs present greater advantages in terms of their lack of toxicity, low cost, easy preparation, and feasibility in terms of luminescence-related applications. Here, two kinds of fluorescent CPDs were obtained through the simple hydrothermal method using biomass avocado peel (CPDs-P) and sarcocarp (CPDs-S) as carbon sources. Interestingly, these two biomass CPDs have excellent applications in ion detection and light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Analysis and results show that CPDs-P possess better sensitivity to Fe3+ because they have more oxygen-containing functional groups. After mixing with epoxy resin, warm and cold white LEDs with CIE (Commission Internationale de L 'Eclairage) coordinates (0.38, 0.39) and (0.29, 0.34) were constructed successfully from extremely stable CPDs-P and CPDs-S. The high color rendering index of the prepared white LEDs are 90.47 and 84.54. This study shows that these biomass CPDs are promising materials in sensing and white LEDs illumination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Hydrothermal method
Ion detection
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of biomass carbonized polymer dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL images of CPDs-P at different excitation wavelengths. (b) The UV–vis spectrum, the maximum excitation spectrum and the maximum emission spectrum of CPDs-P. The inset is the image of the CPDs-P aqueous solution under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light. (c) Fluorescence lifetime diagram of CPDs-P. (d) PL images of CPDs-S at different excitation wavelengths. (e) The UV–vis spectrum, the maximum excitation spectrum and the maximum emission spectrum of CPDs-S. The inset picture is the picture of CPDs-S aqueous solution under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light. (f) Fluorescence lifetime diagram of CPDs-S.

TEM (a), HRTEM (b) and particle size distribution diagram (c) of CPDs-P. TEM (d), HRTEM (e) and particle size distribution diagram (f) of CPDs-S. AFM images of CPDs-P (g) and CPDs-S (h). (i) The height distribution of CPDs-P (top) and CPDs-S (down).
XRD (a), FTIR (b) and XPS (c) of CPDs-P and CPDs-S. The C1s spectrum (d), O1s spectrum (e) and N1s spectrum (f) of CPDs-P. The C1s spectrum (g), O1s spectrum (h) and N1s spectrum (i) of CPDs-S.

The fluorescence emission spectrum (a) and fluorescence intensity graph (b) of CPDs-P after adding different metal ions. The fluorescence emission spectrum (c) and fluorescence intensity graph (d) of CPDs-S after adding different metal ions. The fluorescence emission spectra of CPDs-P solutions with different concentrations of Fe3+ added (e), the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of CPDs-P solution at 0–10 um Fe3+ concentration and Fe3+ ion concentration (f).
The photoluminescence photograph, emission spectrum and the color coordinates (CIE) of the CPDs-P (a), CPDs-S (b) phosphor-based LEDs; and CPDs-P (c), CPDs-S (d) based WLEDs.
Schematic of the preparation of CPDs-P and CPDs-S using avocado as a carbon source.

head.ce:title: Engineering white light-emitting diodes with high color rendering index from biomass carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center, and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Green Catalysis Center, and College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Green Catalysis Center, and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, we prepared CPDs-P with blue light and CPDs-S with blue-green light using the peel and sarcocarp of avocado respectively. The prepared CPDs all have size uniformity. After mixing with epoxy resin, blue LED and blue-green LED are obtained respectively. The red and green CPDs in the laboratory were mixed with two CPDs compounds, and white LEDs were successfully prepared by trying different mass ratios.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have promise in the fields of sensing, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices due to their excellent optical properties, favorable biocompatibility, and superior stability. Biomass CPDs present greater advantages in terms of their lack of toxicity, low cost, easy preparation, and feasibility in terms of luminescence-related applications. Here, two kinds of fluorescent CPDs were obtained through the simple hydrothermal method using biomass avocado peel (CPDs-P) and sarcocarp (CPDs-S) as carbon sources. Interestingly, these two biomass CPDs have excellent applications in ion detection and light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Analysis and results show that CPDs-P possess better sensitivity to Fe3+ because they have more oxygen-containing functional groups. After mixing with epoxy resin, warm and cold white LEDs with CIE (Commission Internationale de L 'Eclairage) coordinates (0.38, 0.39) and (0.29, 0.34) were constructed successfully from extremely stable CPDs-P and CPDs-S. The high color rendering index of the prepared white LEDs are 90.47 and 84.54. This study shows that these biomass CPDs are promising materials in sensing and white LEDs illumination.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are excellent fluorescent materials and mainstream for next generation illumination and display technology. However, conventional semiconductor QDs made of group II-VI elements are generally composed of hypertoxic heavy metal elements (including Cd and Pb) with poor water solubility, weak biological compatibility, and complex preparation. These features limited development and applications [1–3]. Hence, novel QDs with nontoxicity, good biocompatibility, and feasible surface modification are important research directions in chemistry and materials.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have attracted much attention due to their excellent optical [4,5] and chemical properties, good biocompatibility, and easy functionalization [6] in recent years [7,8] More importantly, booming development has been achieved with CPDs in many applications including sensing, biomarking, optoelectronic devices [9,10], bioimaging [11,12], and others [13–15]. In contrast to traditional QDs, CPDs possess superiority in terms of biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [2,16]. The emergence of biomass CPDs can turn the green, renewable, and low-cost carbon sources of various vegetables and fruits into CPDs with optimally optical properties; this further promotes the practicability in bioscience and optoelectronics. Biomass is simple, green, and renewable compared to traditional man-made carbon sources [17]. Previous studies have shown that grapes, orange juice, orange peels, eggs, strawberry juice, soy milk, silk, honey, leaves, and other eco-friendly biomass can be utilized as green carbon sources to prepare biomass CPDs [18–24]. Due to the elemental richness in biomass, trace elements and heteroatoms of the biomass will accumulate on the surface to form abundant functional groups for valuable applications [25]. Thus, biomass CPDs are widely popular in catalysis, biosensor, bioimaging, photoelectricity and many other fields due to their rich heteroatom doping and excellent biocompatibility [23,26–29].

Light emitting diodes (LEDs) with high color rendering index (CRI) are widely featured in commercial illumination [30–32]. CPDs are powerful alternatives and show great performance in LEDs due to their wide peak width at half height of photoluminescence properties and easily controllable emission colors. These are both favorable factors for high CRI LEDs engineering [33]. However, till now, few reports relate the construction of LEDs with feasible biomass CPDs [34].

In this work, two biomass carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-P and CPDs-S) were prepared via a simple green hydrothermal reaction using avocado as a carbon source. The as-prepared CPDs showed bright blue and blue-green fluorescence, size uniformity, and excellent solubility and stability; the surface was highly functionalized with oxygen as well as nitrogen-rich functional groups. Then CPDs-P and CPDs-S are applied to Fe3+ detection and LEDs. The results show that the CPDs-P exhibit excellent Fe3+ sensitivity due to its higher oxygen content and can serve as Fe3+ fluorescent probes. When mixed with epoxy resin, the blue and blue-green LEDs are constructed. After further mixing with red and green CPDs powders that we prepared previously, the white LEDs (WLEDs) were successfully prepared with high CRI (90.47, 84.54). The biomass CPDs here possess great promise in sensing and LEDs applications.

Avocado was purchased from Yonghui Supermarket; CuCl2, BaCl2, NiCl2, CdCl2, FeCl3, Co(NO3)2, CrCl2, ZnCl2, MnSO4, ZnSO4, CaCl2, and MgSO4 were purchased from Sinopharmacological Reagent Co., LTD. All of the chemicals were used directly without further purification. The redistilled water used in this experiment was purified via the SZ-93A water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass carbonized polymer dots
ce:section.ce:para: We weighed 2 g of avocado peel and sarcocarp into a 25-mL PTFE-lined autoclave. This was then heated in an oven at 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C, or 220 °C for 4–8 h. After the autoclave is naturally cooled to room temperature, the reacted substances were removed and vacuum filtered to obtain a brown solution. This was then filtered through a 0.22-μm filter membrane. This was finally dialyzed with a 500-Da membrane for 24 h to remove small molecules. The solution is avocado peel carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-P) and sarcocarp carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-S). The CPDs powder was further obtained by freeze-drying and stored at 4 °C, which can be re-dispersed in deionized water for further characterization.
For more experimental details, please refer to the Supporting Information.

Here, two kinds of fluorescent biomass CPDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal reaction via avocado peel (CPDs-P) and sarcocarp (CPDs-S); they had blue and blue-green emission, respectively (Fig. 1 b, e). The detailed reaction process is presented in Scheme 1 . To achieve high quantity biomasses CPDs, we studied the effect of gradient reaction temperatures and times on the fluorescent properties of CPDs (Figure S1). The fluorescent intensity curve shows that when the reaction time is fixed to 4 h, the fluorescent intensity increases as the reaction temperature increases. After exceeding 200 °C, there is no further enhancement in the intensity (Figure S1a, c). Similarly, when keep the reaction temperature is 200 °C, the fluorescent intensity curve indicates that the fluorescence increases but plateaus at 6 h (Figure S1b, d). Considering the energy consumption and economy, the hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 6 h shows optimal conditions. The detection of structure information of the two CPDs by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) confirms that both CPDs have a quasi-spherical structure with perfect crystal lattice structures. These were uniformly distributed under such reaction conditions (Fig. 2 a, b, d, e). The particle size distributions of the two CPDs are similar with diameters of 2.8 nm and 3.0 nm, respectively (Fig. 2c, f). The lattice fringes are all 0.21 nm (Fig. 2b, e), which all correspond to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [35]. The atomic force microscope (AFM) images (Fig. 2h-i) indicate that the heights of CPDs-P and CPDs-S are 4 nm and 4.5 nm, respectively, corresponding to eight to ten layers of graphene structure [36]. X-Ray diffraction (XRD) data (Fig. 3 a) shows that both CPDs display a wide peak near 22°, which is a characteristic peak of a typical CPDs and corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite corresponding to TEM data [37–39]. Thus, two kinds of fluorescent biomass CPDs are effectively synthesized here thus the optical properties are explored further below.

The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1b, e) of these two CPDs both show two exaction absorption bands at about 275 nm and 325 nm. They are typically attributed to the ᴨ→ᴨ* transition of aromatic carbon and the n→ᴨ* transition between the oxygen/nitrogen group and the sp2 domain, respectively. By adjusting the wavelength of the excitation light, the emission spectra of CPDs-P (Fig. 1a) and CPDs-S (Fig. 1d) both show obvious excitation dependence. The optimal excitation wavelength of CPDs-P is 380 nm, and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength is 480 nm (Fig. 1b). The optimal excitation wavelength of CPDs-S is 420 nm, and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength of CPDs-S is 508 nm (Fig. 1e). Their aqueous solutions show bright blue and blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (Fig. 1b, e). The fluorescence lifetimes of CPDs-P (Fig. 1c) and CPDs-S (Fig. 1f) were measured via time-dependent single photon counting. The average lifetime of CPDs-P and CPDs-S are calculated to be 2.98 ns and 4.74 ns indicating that these two CPDs only have fluorescent properties. The absolute fluorescence QYs calculated using the integrating sphere are 9.56% and 8.97%, respectively, which are comparable in biomass CPDs field.

Careful analysis of the PL decay information is performed to ascertain the origin and internal factors that trigger the multicolor emissions. The time-resolved PL decay curves of the different emission of the two CPDs are measured under excitation at 340 nm (Fig. 2c, f). The fluorescence intensity decay graph shows the kinetics of electron inactivation from the excited state to the ground state through radiation and non-radiation pathways. The curves observed here can be fitted to two indices similar to those in previous reports [14]. Table S1 summarizes the lifetime, overall contribution, and complete fitting parameters. Both decays include a fast component (τ1) due to the radiative recombination of the eigenstate and two slower components (τ2) due to surface functional groups. The results of these two CPDs show that the average lifetime are 4.74 ns and 2.98 ns, and τ1 are 51.05% and 75.70% indicating that the core state and surface groups in the radiation lifetime of these two CPDs play a role together. The sizes of carbon core are same, and thus the surface state here plays a dominant role in emission variation.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are powerful characterization methods to the surface structures located on the carbonized core. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b) of CPDs-P and CPDs-S show the same stretching vibration absorption peak at 3430 cm−1, 1653 cm−1, and 1126 cm−1 corresponding to the OH/NH, CO, and CN [40,41]. The FTIR data indicates that the surface of the two CPDs contain carboxyl, amino, and other functional groups. The XPS spectrum in Fig. 3c shows that CPDs-P and CPDs-S contain the same elements. The C 1s band has three component peaks corresponding to the sp2 carbon (CC, 284.8 eV), sp3 carbon (CO/CN, 285.9 eV), and carbonyl group (CO, 288.3 eV). The N 1s band also has two component peaks, 398.7 and 400.3 eV, corresponding to pyridine N and graphitic N, respectively [26,42]. The two‐component O 1s peaks at 531.2 and 532.3 eV arose from CO and CO. A detailed analysis showed that the graphitic N in CPDs-S is much more than in CPDs-P (Table S2), which is the primary factor for the relative bathochromic shift in emission compared to CPDs-P.

In addition, the data from XPS and FTIR show that the surface of these two CPDs has abundant functional groups, which increases their water solubility. The functional groups on the surface can better bind to ions and promote their ion detection capabilities. Stability tests were conducted for ion detection with these two CPDs. First, hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were used to adjust the pH value of the prepared CPDs aqueous solution and to compare the fluorescence spectra of the two samples under different pH conditions [43]. Figure S2 shows when the pH is 4–11, the fluorescence intensity changes of the two CPDs are very small. The fluorescence intensity of both drop sharply in a strong acid environment with a pH of 2–4 and a strong alkali environment with a pH of 11–13. The pH value of the physiological environment is generally 7–8, indicating that the two types of CPDs prepared can be used in biological sensing and imaging. Subsequently, the stability of these two CPDs to NaCl solution was further tested. Figure S3 shows that the NaCl concentration increased from 0.2 mol L−1 to 1.0 mol L−1, and the fluorescence intensity of the two CPDs did not change significantly indicating that both have excellent stability to NaCl. In contrast to the poor light stability of traditional fluorescent dyes, CPDs usually have good fluorescence emission stability, which can solve many inconveniences of traditional fluorescent dyes during storage and use.

Finally, the UV stability of the CPDs were tested. Figure S4 shows that the fluorescence intensity of the two CPDs do not change significantly after continuous irradiation of 150 W ultraviolet light (365 nm) for 6 h indicating that both of them have high ultraviolet light stability. These results indicate that the two CPDs prepared here have high stability against NaCl and ultraviolet lightand suitable pH stability. This makes them more conducive to ion detection.

Ion detection on CPDs-P and CPDs-S were performed next. The surface of the CPDs contains oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, which can interact with metal ions through coordination to form complex compounds, so that the CPDs have the potential to be used as fluorescent probes for detecting metal ions. Because Fe3+ ion has a half-field 3d shell, making it a good electron acceptor, it is believed that the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ is related to the rapid electron transfer process between Fe3+ and CPDs. There is no electron transfer between the CPDs and other metal ions, so the quenching effect of other metal ions is almost negligible [19,23,26,27]. The sensing capability of the prepared CPDs was tested by measuring fluorescence intensity after adding various metal salts (5 mmol L−1 GSH, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, K+, Glc, Na+ and Zn2+) [44]. Fig. 4 a shows that CPDs-P has good selectivity to Fe3+. Fe3+ with a concentration of 5 mmol L−1 can quench over 90% of the fluorescence of CPDs-P while other common metal ions will not quench the fluorescence of CPDs-P. Fig. 4c shows the absorption spectra of CPDs-S in different metal ions. The CPDs-S also have certain selectivity to Fe3+. Fe3+ (5 mmol L−1) can make the fluorescence quenching of CPDs-S 60% while other common metal ions will not quench the fluorescence of CPDs-S. Since CPDs-P has more negative charges, it is more likely to bind to Fe3+. Therefore, CPDs-P has a better quenching effect on Fe3+.

FTIR analysis (Fig. 3b) shows that the NH/OH absorption peak of CPDs-P was stronger than CPDs-S, which may induce a negative charge on the surface of CPDs-P and can be used to detect Fe3+ ions. The negative charge in CPDs-P may cause the selectivity to be more accurate. In addition, in the XPS curve (Fig. 3c), the contents of C, N, and O in CPDs-P are 83.41%, 1.12%, and 15.47%, respectively. The contents of C, N, and O in CPDs-S are 80.54%, 5.87%, and 13.59%, respectively. The oxygen content in CPDs-P is significantly higher than that in CPDs-S indicating that there are more oxygen-containing groups in CPDs-P, which are more conducive to binding with Fe3+. The XPS results are consistent with FTIR results indicating that CPDs-P contains more oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface than CPDs-S. To further verify the difference between CPDs-S and CPDs-P, zeta potential tests were conducted on these two CPDs. We found that the Zeta potential of CPDs-P (−15.9 mV) is indeed more negative (Figure S5) while the Zeta potential of CPDs-S is −9.73 mV (Figure S6). This indicates that the surface states of CPDs-P and CPDs-S are different, which may result in different sensitivity to different ions. These results clearly demonstrate the efficacy of these two CPDs as fluorescence sensors. These are more selective to Fe3+ than other metal ions. Moreover, the selectivity of CPDs-P to Fe3+ is higher than that of CPDs-S.

Therefore, the detailed sensor response of CPDs-P to Fe3+ ion was further studied. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 0 to 10 μmol L−1 (Fig. 4e). In addition, within a certain range of Fe3+ concentrations, there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CPDs-P solution and the concentration of Fe3+ with a linear relationship coefficient R2 = 0.994. Overall, these results clearly indicate that the specific effect of Fe3+ ions on CPDs fluorescence makes CPDs potentially useful for selective sensing of Fe3+ ions.

There are many applications of biomass CPDs in ion detection, but few in LEDs applications [2,8,45]. To broaden its application value, WLEDs are prepared by mixing them with epoxy resin. Due to the excellent stability and unique optical properties of CPDs [46], the two CPDs fluorescence emission peaks prepared both fall in the blue region and have relatively wide peak width. They can thus be better applied as LEDs illumination materials [47]. The two CPDs are compounded with epoxy resin and dropped onto the chip with a luminescence center of 365 nm to make LEDs lamp beads. Under UV irradiation of 365 nm, the complex can still emit blue fluorescence, and its emission spectrum was recorded and plotted in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence spectrum showed no significant deviation compared to the two CPDs solutions. The LEDs constructed by the CPDs-P powder is shown in Fig. 5a–c. It emits bright blue light during operation with the CIE color coordinate at (0.22, 0.24) and CRI (Color Rendering Index) value of 80.58.

The LEDs constructed from CPDs-S powder is shown in Fig. 5d–f. It also emits bright blue light during operation. The CIE coordinates are located at (0.23, 0.27), and the CRI value is 78.63. The WLEDs, especially warm WLEDs, are more efficient because they use less power and have been widely studied. Red, green, and blue can be mixed into white. In the laboratory, red light and green light CPDs can be mixed with the two CPDs compounds at varying quality ratios to successful prepare WLEDs.

A photo of a WLEDs constructed from CPDs-P powder is shown in Fig. 5g-i. The CIE coordinate is located at (0.38, 0.39), and the CRI is 90.47. The photo of the LEDs built by CPDs-S powder is shown in Fig. 5j–l. It also emits bright warm white light during operation. The CIE coordinates are located at (0.29, 0.34), the color temperature is 4088 K, and the CRI value is 84.54. These results show that white light LEDs and multicolor LEDs can be prepared by controlling the ratio of CPDs to epoxy resin. Compared to LEDs prepared by other organic molecules, the white light LEDs prepared by biomass CPDs are easy to operate (Table S3). Compared to traditional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and inorganic phosphors prepared from rare earth elements, the CPDs prepared here based on biomass can significantly reduce the production cost of phosphors to achieve cheap LEDs for practical and commercial illumination.

In conclusion, two kinds of CPDs (CPDs-P, CPDs-S) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal reaction with avocado as the carbon source. CPDs-P possesses bright blue fluorescence while CPDs-S has a bright blue-green fluorescence. These two CPDs are uniformly dispersed and have a quasi-spherical structure. The surface has many functional groups, which is more conducive to coordination with Fe3+. It also has good detection ability. More importantly, these two CPDs powders can also be used to prepare WLEDs with high CRI (90.47 and 84.54, respectively), exceeding most CPDs prepared with inorganic molecules and polymer precursors. Compared to traditional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots or inorganic phosphors prepared from other rare earth materials, white light has the advantages of being inexpensive and easy to obtain and has excellent properties. Preparing CPDs directly from biomass not only simplifies the production process of phosphors but also greatly reduces the cost, which shows great potential in the practical application of LEDs in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21905253, 51973200), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640681, 2019T120632) and Natural Science Foundation of Henan (202300410372).

Weixue Meng and Boyang Wang contributed to the synthesis and characterization. Weixue Meng and Haoqiang Song wrote the manuscript. Lin Ai organized the manuscript. Siyu Lu conceived the idea and organized the manuscript. All the authors contributed to discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.04.022.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots: bright and versatile in vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging biosensors
